## 4.2 Concept Map — The Six Standard 3D View Directions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WC[World Coordinate System Origin] --> N1[Top View: VPOINT 0,0,1]
    WC --> N2[Front View: VPOINT 0,-1,0]
    WC --> N3[Right View: VPOINT 1,0,0]
    WC --> N4[SE Isometric: VPOINT 1,-1,1]
    WC --> N5[SW Isometric: VPOINT -1,-1,1]
    WC --> N6[NE Isometric: VPOINT 1,1,1]
    WC --> N7[Bottom View: VPOINT 0,0,-1]
    WC --> N8[Back View: VPOINT 0,1,0]
    style WC fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style N4 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333
    style N5 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333
    style N6 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Boolean Operation Truth Table (KTU High-Yield)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph B[Boolean Operations Between Two Solids A and B]
    direction TB
    U[UNION: A + B<br/>Result contains volume of both]
    S[SUBTRACT: A - B<br/>Result is A with B removed]
    I[INTERSECT: A ∩ B<br/>Result is common volume only]
    end
    A[Solid A] --> B
    B --> U
    B --> S
    B --> I
    B2[Solid B] --> B
    style U fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style S fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333
    style I fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Assessment Structure for PCPOL308 (CAD Lab)**
> - **Internal Continuous Evaluation (CE)**: 50 marks (Lab performance, records, viva)
> - **End Semester Examination (ESE)**: 50 marks (Practical exam with viva voce)
> - Questions are framed on the 2024 scheme Bloom's Taxonomy: **Apply (L3), Analyze (L4), Create (L5)**.

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between 2D drafting and 3D drafting. List any three 3D primitive commands available in AutoCAD.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | 2D Drafting | 3D Drafting |
|---|---|---|
| **Axes Used** | Only X and Y | X, Y, and Z |
| **Output** | Flat drawings (plan, elevation) | Solid/surface/wireframe models |
| **Viewpoints** | Fixed (top/plan) | Rotatable to any angle via VPOINT / 3DORBIT |
| **Information** | Length and Width only | Length, Width, and Height (depth) |
| **Operations** | TRIM, EXTEND, OFFSET | EXTRUDE, REVOLVE, UNION, SUBTRACT |

**Three 3D Primitive Commands:**
1. **BOX** — Creates a rectangular cuboid solid.
2. **CYLINDER** — Creates a solid cylinder from center, radius, and height.
3. **SPHERE** — Creates a solid sphere from center and radius.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the function of the following commands: (a) UCS, (b) SUBTRACT, (c) MASSPROP.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) UCS (User Coordinate System):**
The `UCS` command is used to relocate and reorient the XY drawing plane (origin and axes) anywhere in 3D space. This is essential when you need to draw a 2D profile on an inclined or vertical face of an existing 3D solid. The default is the **World Coordinate System (WCS)**.

**(b) SUBTRACT:**
The `SUBTRACT` command is a **Boolean operation** that removes the volume of one or more solids (the "tool" objects) from another solid (the "source" object). It is used to create holes, notches, and cutouts in a 3D model.
Syntax: `SUBTRACT → Select source → Enter → Select tool(s) → Enter`

**(c) MASSPROP:**
The `MASSPROP` command analyzes a 3D solid and reports its **physical properties** — volume, surface area, centroid (X, Y, Z), moments of inertia, radii of gyration, and principal axes. It is used to verify the correctness of a 3D model dimensionally.

---

## 📝 Part B — Detailed Questions (1 × 14 = 14 Marks) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question 3A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` (CHOICE A)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the procedure to draw a **stepped block (L-shape)** of overall size 100 mm × 80 mm × 60 mm (Length × Width × Height) with a 40 mm × 40 mm × 30 mm step cut from one of its top corners. Use appropriate commands. (Use BOX and SUBTRACT.)

**(b) [7 Marks]** A solid cylinder of diameter 80 mm and height 100 mm is to be drawn with a coaxial hole of diameter 50 mm drilled completely through it. List the commands, their sequence, and the expected volume after the boolean operation.

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question 3A(a) — Stepped L-Block

**Given Data:**
- Outer dimensions: 100 (X) × 80 (Y) × 60 (Z) mm
- Notch to be cut: 40 (X) × 40 (Y) × 30 (Z) mm from top-right corner

**2D Blueprint:**

```
  Top View (Z = 0 to 60):
  +--------+--------+
  |        |        |
  |        |        |  ← Y direction = 80 mm
  |  100   |   40   |
  |  X 60  |   X    |  ← Notch height = 30 mm
  |        |        |
  +--------+--------+
       X = 100 mm total
```

**Procedure:**

1. **Set units:** `UNITS` → Decimal → Millimeters.
2. **Set view:** `VPOINT` → `1,-1,1` (SE Isometric).
3. **Create Base Box:**
   - Command: `BOX`
   - First corner: `0,0,0`
   - Opposite corner: `100,80`
   - Height: `60`
4. **Create Notch Box:**
   - Command: `BOX`
   - First corner: `60,40,60` *(Top face starts at Z=60; notch occupies the +X+Y corner.)*
   - Opposite corner: `100,80`
   - Height: `30` *(Extends 30 mm above the base, removing a 30-mm-tall section.)*
5. **Subtract:** `SUBTRACT` → Select base box → Enter → Select notch box → Enter.
6. **Visualize:** Use `CONCEPTUAL` or `REALISTIC` visual style.
7. **Verify:** `MASSPROP` → Volume should be:
   $$V = (100 \times 80 \times 60) - (40 \times 40 \times 30)$$
   $$V = 480{,}000 - 48{,}000 = 432{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$$

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question 3A(b) — Drilled Cylinder

**Given Data:**
- Outer cylinder: Diameter = 80 mm → Radius **R = 40 mm**, Height **H = 100 mm**
- Inner hole: Diameter = 50 mm → Radius **r = 25 mm**, Depth = 100 mm (through)

**Procedure:**

1. **Create Outer Cylinder:**
   - `CYLINDER` → Center: `0,0,0` → Radius: `40` → Height: `100`
2. **Create Inner Hole Cylinder:**
   - `CYLINDER` → Center: `0,0,0` → Radius: `25` → Height: `100`
3. **Subtract:**
   - `SUBTRACT` → Select outer cylinder → Enter → Select inner cylinder → Enter
4. **View in Isometric:** `VPOINT` → `1,-1,1`, then `HIDE`.

**Volume Calculation:**

$$V_{\text{outer}} = \pi R^2 H = \pi \times 40^2 \times 100 = 160{,}000 \pi \text{ mm}^3$$

$$V_{\text{hole}} = \pi r^2 H = \pi \times 25^2 \times 100 = 62{,}500 \pi \text{ mm}^3$$

$$V_{\text{net}} = V_{\text{outer}} - V_{\text{hole}} = (160{,}000 - 62{,}500) \pi$$

$$V_{\text{net}} = 97{,}500 \pi \approx 306{,}305.92 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Commands Summary:**

| Step | Command | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `CYLINDER` | Draw outer solid |
| 2 | `CYLINDER` | Draw inner tool solid |
| 3 | `SUBTRACT` | Remove inner from outer |
| 4 | `VPOINT 1,-1,1` | Switch to 3D view |
| 5 | `HIDE` | Hide hidden lines |
| 6 | `MASSPROP` | Verify volume ≈ 306,306 mm³ |

---

### **Question 3B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` (CHOICE B)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the procedure to draw a **hollow sphere with a square box cut-out** passing through its center. Use the sphere radius as 50 mm and the box cross-section as 30 × 30 mm.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **six standard isometric viewpoints** in AutoCAD and their VPOINT coordinates. Why is the SE Isometric (1, -1, 1) used as the default 3D view?

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question 3B(a) — Sphere with Box Cut-Out

**Procedure:**

1. **Set view:** `VPOINT` → `1,-1,1`
2. **Create Sphere:**
   - `SPHERE` → Center: `0,0,0` → Radius: `50`
3. **Create Cut-Out Box (must be longer than sphere diameter to ensure a through-cut):**
   - `BOX` → First corner: `-15,-15,-100`
   - Opposite corner: `@30,30,0` *(creates 30×30 base at Z=-100)*
   - Height: `200` *(ensures the box passes fully through the 100 mm diameter sphere)*
4. **Subtract the Box from the Sphere:**
   - `SUBTRACT` → Select sphere → Enter → Select box → Enter
5. **Visualize:** `REALISTIC` style → `3DORBIT` to inspect.

**Volume Calculation:**

$$V_{\text{sphere}} = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 = \frac{4}{3} \pi \times 50^3 = \frac{500{,}000}{3} \pi$$

$$V_{\text{sphere}} \approx 523{,}598.78 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$V_{\text{box (intersecting sphere)}} = 30 \times 30 \times 100 = 90{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$V_{\text{net}} = 523{,}598.78 - 90{,}000 \approx 433{,}598.78 \text{ mm}^3$$

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question 3B(b) — Six Isometric Viewpoints

**Table of Viewpoints:**

| S.No. | View Name | VPOINT Coordinates | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Top / Plan | `(0, 0, 1)` | Looking down the +Z axis |
| 2 | Bottom | `(0, 0, -1)` | Looking up from below |
| 3 | Front / Elevation | `(0, -1, 0)` | Looking along -Y axis |
| 4 | Back | `(0, 1, 0)` | Looking along +Y axis |
| 5 | Right Side | `(1, 0, 0)` | Looking along +X axis |
| 6 | Left Side | `(-1, 0, 0)` | Looking along -X axis |
| 7 | **SE Isometric** (Default) | `(1, -1, 1)` | Standard 3D view |
| 8 | NE Isometric | `(1, 1, 1)` | Rotated isometric |
| 9 | SW Isometric | `(-1, -1, 1)` | Mirrored isometric |

**Why SE Isometric is the default:**
The SE Isometric viewpoint `(1, -1, 1)` is equidistant from the three axes (magnitude = √3), which produces an **isometric projection** — all three axes appear at exactly **120° from each other** and foreshortening is uniform. This gives the most balanced and natural 3D appearance of an object, which is why AutoCAD and most CAD packages use it as the default 3D view.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to switch from 2D to 3D view:** Students often draw 3D primitives in **Top View**, making the model look flat in the printout. Always invoke `VPOINT 1,-1,1` and `HIDE` (or change to `CONCEPTUAL` visual style) before taking screenshots.
> 2. **Wrong corner selection in BOX:** When specifying the second corner of a BOX using `@` (relative) coordinates, the height input applies to the **Z direction**. Typing `@100,100` without entering height creates only a rectangle, not a 3D box.
> 3. **Reversing SUBTRACT order:** If you select the small box first (as source) and the large box as the tool, you will end up with **only the small box** in the model. Always select the **larger/main solid as the source** and the **cutting object as the tool**.
> 4. **Confusing 2D commands with 3D commands:** `TRIM` and `EXTEND` work on 2D entities. To trim 3D solid edges, use `FILLETEDGE` or `CHAMFEREDGE`.
> 5. **Not using MASSPROP for verification:** KTU examiners often deduct 1–2 marks if you do not verify the model dimensions using `MASSPROP` and cross-check with hand calculation.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> 📋 **High-Density Quick Revision — Pin this to your memory before the lab exam!**

- [x] **3D = X + Y + Z** — The third axis is **Z** (height/depth), absent in 2D.
- [x] **Right-Hand Rule** determines the positive direction of the Z-axis.
- [x] **Seven 3D Primitives** in AutoCAD: `BOX`, `WEDGE`, `CYLINDER`, `CONE`, `SPHERE`, `TORUS`, `PYRAMID`.
- [x] **2D → 3D conversion commands**: `EXTRUDE`, `REVOLVE`, `SWEEP`, `LOFT`.
- [x] **Three Boolean Operations**: `UNION` (add), `SUBTRACT` (cut), `INTERSECT` (common volume).
- [x] **Viewing commands**: `VPOINT 1,-1,1` (SE Iso), `3DORBIT`, `VIEW` (SE Iso), `HIDE`.
- [x] **UCS** = User Coordinate System. Used to redraw the XY "ground plane" on any face of a 3D solid.
- [x] **VPOINT coordinates of the 6 orthographic views**: Top `(0,0,1)`, Bottom `(0,0,-1)`, Front `(0,-1,0)`, Back `(0,1,0)`, Right `(1,0,0)`, Left `(-1,0,0)`.
- [x] **Torus formula**: Volume = **2π²Rr²** (R = major, r = minor radius).
- [x] **Sphere volume**: **V = (4/3)πr³**.
- [x] **Cylinder volume**: **V = πr²h**.
- [x] **MASSPROP** is the most reliable verification tool for 3D solid accuracy.
- [x] **Visual styles**: Wireframe → Hidden → Conceptual → Realistic (in order of increasing photorealism).
- [x] **Edge treatment commands**: `FILLETEDGE` (rounds edges), `CHAMFEREDGE` (bevels edges).
- [x] **3D Editing commands**: `3DMOVE`, `3DROTATE`, `MIRROR3D`, `3DARRAY` (Rectangular / Polar).
- [x] **Default view for 3D**: SE Isometric `(1,-1,1)` is universally accepted as the standard 3D viewing angle.
- [x] **Always end with `REGEN`** after switching views to refresh the display.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Draft Various Polymer Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL308 - COMPUTER AIDED DRAFTING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design and Draft Various Polymer Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Polymer Product Design and Drafting** is the systematic engineering process of conceptualizing, dimensionally defining, and graphically representing polymer-based articles (such as containers, pipes, housings, gears, bottles, caps, and fittings) using **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)** platforms, while strictly conforming to the manufacturing constraints of polymer processing techniques like **Injection Molding, Blow Molding, Extrusion, Thermoforming, and Compression Molding**.

In the context of **PCPOL308 – Computer Aided Drafting Lab**, Experiment 2 demands that the student translate real-world polymer artifacts into orthographic projections, isometric views, sectional views, and 3D solid models using CAD software (typically **AutoCAD / SolidWorks / CATIA / Fusion 360 / Creo**) while embedding process-specific design intelligence into the geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 2):** Students must demonstrate the ability to design and draft *at least 4–6 diverse polymer products* — covering categories like hollow containers, threaded closures, pipe fittings, injection-molded housings, and rotational-molded tanks — with proper dimensioning, GD&T, material specifications, and shrinkage allowance integration.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chef preparing a jelly dessert in a mold**. The mold is your CAD model, the jelly is the molten polymer, and the recipe (temperature, cooling time, ingredient ratios) is your design rulebook. If the mold has sharp internal corners, the jelly will not flow smoothly and will crack at the corners. If the mold walls are too thick, the jelly cools unevenly and warps. If the mold lacks a slight taper (draft), you cannot pull the jelly out without breaking it.

**CAD drafting of polymer products is exactly this:** you are designing the "mold cavity" virtually so that the real polymer, once injected, blown, or extruded, will:
1. **Fill** the cavity completely,
2. **Cool uniformly** without internal stresses,
3. **Eject** from the mold without distortion, and
4. **Perform its function** (hold water, insulate, transmit force, etc.) reliably over its service life.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** A polymer product is never just a shape — it is a *shape constrained by physics*. Every wall, rib, boss, and draft angle exists to defeat a specific failure mode (warpage, sink marks, short shots, ejection damage).

## 1.3 Core Vocabulary You Must Master

| Term | Definition | Typical Value (Bold Constants) |
|------|------------|-------------------------------|
| **Shrinkage Allowance** | Extra dimension added to the mold to compensate for polymer contraction on cooling. | **0.2% – 3%** depending on resin |
| **Draft Angle** | Taper applied to vertical walls to allow part ejection. | **0.5° – 2°** (1° typical) |
| **Wall Thickness** | Nominal distance between outer skin and inner skin of a molded part. | **1 mm – 6 mm** (typical) |
| **Rib** | Internal strengthening feature projecting from a wall. | **0.5× – 0.8×** nominal wall thickness |
| **Boss** | Cylindrical protrusion for receiving a fastener or insert. | **2× – 3×** hole diameter |
| **Fillet Radius** | Internal curved transition between two surfaces. | **0.5× – 0.8×** wall thickness |
| **Parting Line** | Trace of mold separation visible on the finished part. | Must be located on a non-aesthetic surface |
| **Gate Mark** | Witness of the polymer entry point into the cavity. | Typically **0.5 mm – 3 mm** diameter |
| **GD&T** | Geometric Dimensioning and Tolerancing per ASME Y14.5. | Tolerances as tight as **± 0.05 mm** |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Design-for-Manufacturing (DFM) Philosophy

Polymer product design is governed by a hierarchy of constraints. When you open a CAD package, every dimension you enter is a decision that will be tested by **viscosity, thermal expansion, and ejection forces**. The five pillars of polymer product design are:

### Pillar 1 — Uniform Wall Thickness
Polymers are thermal insulators. A thick section cools slower than a thin section, creating **differential shrinkage**, which manifests as:
- **Sink marks** on cosmetic surfaces,
- **Voids** inside the bulk,
- **Residual stresses** that warp the part over time.

The golden rule is:
$$\frac{t_{\max}}{t_{\min}} \leq 2 \quad \text{(ideally } \leq 1.5\text{)}$$

### Pillar 2 — Draft Angles (Ejection Geometry)
When a molded part cools, it grips the mold core. Without a draft angle, ejection force becomes infinite. A draft angle converts the ejection problem from **sliding friction** to **near-zero-friction normal separation**.

For a wall of height $h$ and projected area $A$, the draft taper $\tan \alpha$ ensures that the part slides off the core as it shrinks:

$$\Delta d = h \cdot \tan \alpha$$

For $\alpha = 1°$ and $h = 100$ mm:
$$\Delta d = 100 \times \tan(1°) = 100 \times 0.01745 \approx 1.745 \text{ mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Always add draft *after* applying the shrinkage factor. Draft is a *manufacturing dimension*; shrinkage is a *material compensation*. The order is: **(1) Design intent → (2) Shrinkage compensation → (3) Draft application → (4) Machining/moldmaker tolerance stack-up.**

### Pillar 3 — Ribs, Bosses & Gussets
Instead of thickening a wall (which causes sinks), engineers add **ribs** to increase stiffness. A rib is essentially a *thin internal wall*. Its thickness must be carefully limited:

$$t_{\text{rib}} = k \cdot t_{\text{wall}}, \quad k \in [0.5,\ 0.8]$$

If a rib is too thick, it acts as a thick wall and causes a sink on the opposite cosmetic face. The **Height-to-Thickness (H/t) ratio** of a rib should be controlled:

$$\frac{H}{t_{\text{rib}}} \leq 3:1 \quad \text{(up to 5:1 with proper base radius)}$$

The base of every rib must have a generous fillet:
$$R_{\text{fillet}} = (0.5 \text{ to } 0.8) \cdot t_{\text{wall}}$$

### Pillar 4 — Boss Design
A boss is a cylindrical hub for a screw, insert, or threaded hole. To prevent sink marks, the boss must be hollowed:

$$D_{\text{boss,outer}} = 2.5 \cdot d_{\text{hole}} \quad \text{(minimum)}$$

The wall around a boss should be reinforced by a **gusset** or **webs**, not by thickening the boss itself.

### Pillar 5 — Shrinkage Compensation
The CAD model represents the **finished part at room temperature**. The mold cavity must be **larger** to account for the polymer shrinking as it cools:

$$D_{\text{mold}} = D_{\text{part}} \times (1 + S)$$

where $S$ is the **volumetric / linear shrinkage fraction**.

| Polymer | Linear Shrinkage (typical) |
|---------|---------------------------|
| Polyethylene (PE) | **1.5% – 3.0%** |
| Polypropylene (PP) | **1.0% – 2.5%** |
| Polystyrene (PS) | **0.2% – 0.6%** |
| Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene (ABS) | **0.4% – 0.7%** |
| Polycarbonate (PC) | **0.5% – 0.7%** |
| Nylon (PA 6,6) | **1.0% – 2.0%** |
| Polyoxymethylene (POM) | **1.8% – 2.5%** |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains the core equations you will need for Part A derivations, Part B numericals, and viva voce in PCPOL308.**

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $D_{\text{mold}} = D_{\text{part}} \cdot (1 + S)$ | Mold cavity dimension after shrinkage | mm |
| 2 | $S = \dfrac{D_{\text{mold}} - D_{\text{part}}}{D_{\text{part}}} \times 100\%$ | Linear shrinkage percentage | % |
| 3 | $\Delta d = h \cdot \tan \alpha$ | Draft-induced dimensional change at height $h$ | mm |
| 4 | $\dfrac{t_{\max}}{t_{\min}} \leq 2$ | Wall thickness uniformity rule | dimensionless |
| 5 | $t_{\text{rib}} = (0.5 \text{ to } 0.8) \cdot t_{\text{wall}}$ | Rib thickness rule | mm |
| 6 | $R_{\text{fillet}} = (0.5 \text{ to } 0.8) \cdot t_{\text{wall}}$ | Base fillet radius rule | mm |
| 7 | $D_{\text{boss}} \geq 2.5 \cdot d_{\text{hole}}$ | Boss outer diameter rule | mm |
| 8 | $V_{\text{part}} = m / \rho$ | Part volume from mass and density | cm³ or mm³ |
| 9 | $A_{\text{projected}} = \sum w_i \cdot h_i$ | Projected area for clamp tonnage | mm² |
| 10 | $F_{\text{clamp}} = A_{\text{projected}} \cdot P_{\text{inj}}$ | Required clamp tonnage | tonnes (kN) |
| 11 | $t_{\text{cycle}} = t_{\text{inj}} + t_{\text{cool}} + t_{\text{eject}}$ | Total cycle time | seconds |
| 12 | $\alpha_{\text{deg}} = \alpha_{\text{rad}} \cdot \dfrac{180}{\pi}$ | Angle unit conversion | degrees |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Typical Polymer Product | Critical Design Driver |
|----------|------------------------|------------------------|
| **Packaging** | PET bottles, HDPE caps | Wall uniformity, neck finish dimensions, lightweighting |
| **Automotive** | Dashboard panels, bumpers, air-intake manifolds | Rib design, boss integrity, clip retention |
| **Piping** | PVC pipe, PP-R fittings, PEX tubing | SDR ratio, pressure rating, socket fusion geometry |
| **Electrical** | Junction boxes, switch plates, connector housings | UL94 flammability, creep resistance, rib stiffening |
| **Consumer Goods** | Bucket, mug, tiffin box, chair | Draft, surface finish, hinge (living hinge) integrity |
| **Medical** | Syringe barrel, IV spike, petri dish | ISO 13485, biocompatibility, tight tolerances |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "design and draft a polymer product," always state the **material choice, shrinkage value, draft direction, and parting line** before you start drawing. Examiners award marks for design *intent*, not just lines on a screen.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Shrinkage Compensation for a Bottle

**Problem (KTU-Style):**
A **1 L HDPE bottle** has a nominal outer diameter of **85 mm** at its mid-body. The molding shop reports a linear shrinkage of **2.0%** for the chosen HDPE grade. Determine the **mold cavity diameter** required to produce the bottle at the nominal dimension.

### Step 1 — Identify Given Data
- Finished part diameter: $D_{\text{part}} = 85$ mm
- Linear shrinkage fraction: $S = 2.0\% = 0.020$

### Step 2 — Recall the Governing Equation
$$D_{\text{mold}} = D_{\text{part}} \cdot (1 + S)$$

### Step 3 — Substitute
$$D_{\text{mold}} = 85 \times (1 + 0.020)$$

### Step 4 — Compute
$$D_{\text{mold}} = 85 \times 1.020$$

$$D_{\text{mold}} = 86.7 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 5 — Interpret
The mold cavity must be machined to **86.7 mm** so that the cooled bottle measures exactly **85.0 mm** ± the agreed tolerance band (typically **± 0.2 mm** for cosmetic containers).

$$\boxed{D_{\text{mold}} = 86.7 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reverse Check:** If the mold is built at 86.7 mm and the part shrinks by 2%, then $86.7 \times (1 - 0.02) = 86.7 \times 0.98 = 84.966$ mm. The tiny discrepancy with 85 mm is due to round-off in the shrinkage fraction. In practice, moldmakers use the more precise form $D_{\text{mold}} = D_{\text{part}} / (1 - S) = 85 / 0.98 = 86.735$ mm, which is **the more rigorous formula**.

Let us re-derive the rigorous form:
$$D_{\text{part}} = D_{\text{mold}} (1 - S)$$

$$D_{\text{mold}} = \frac{D_{\text{part}}}{1 - S} = \frac{85}{1 - 0.020} = \frac{85}{0.980}$$

$$D_{\text{mold}} = 86.7347 \text{ mm}$$

This is the form used by mold designers in industry.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Draft Angle and Ejection Geometry

**Problem:**
A cylindrical **PP container** has a wall height of **120 mm**. A draft angle of **1°** is applied to the inner wall (textured surface). Determine (a) the increase in inner diameter at the top relative to the bottom, and (b) the new inner diameter if the bottom inner diameter is **70 mm**.

### Step 1 — Identify Data
- Wall height: $h = 120$ mm
- Draft angle: $\alpha = 1°$
- Bottom inner diameter: $d_{\text{bottom}} = 70$ mm

### Step 2 — Compute the Draft-Induced Diameter Increase
Because draft is applied on **both sides** of a cylindrical wall (or as a single-sided taper with the opposite wall remaining straight, depending on the mold construction), we consider the standard case of a uniform taper:

$$\Delta d = h \cdot \tan \alpha = 120 \times \tan(1°)$$

$$\tan(1°) = 0.017455$$

$$\Delta d = 120 \times 0.017455 = 2.0946 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Top Diameter
$$d_{\text{top}} = d_{\text{bottom}} + 2 \cdot \Delta d$$

(The factor of 2 is needed because the diameter changes due to taper on both sides of the axis, i.e., a total radial movement of $\Delta d$ on each side.)

$$d_{\text{top}} = 70 + 2 \times 2.0946 = 70 + 4.1892$$

$$d_{\text{top}} = 74.189 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 4 — Summary

$$\boxed{\Delta d = 2.095 \text{ mm}, \quad d_{\text{top}} = 74.19 \text{ mm}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Wall Thickness Uniformity Audit

**Problem:**
A proposed **ABS electronic enclosure** has the following wall sections (in mm):
- Side wall: **3.0**
- Top boss boss-pedestal: **8.0** (around a mounting boss)
- Rib base: **2.5**

Check whether the design satisfies the uniform-wall-thickness rule. If not, propose a corrective design change.

### Step 1 — Compute the Thickness Ratios
$$R_1 = \frac{t_{\text{boss}}}{t_{\text{wall}}} = \frac{8.0}{3.0} = 2.67$$

$$R_2 = \frac{t_{\text{wall}}}{t_{\text{rib}}} = \frac{3.0}{2.5} = 1.20$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Rule
The rule is $\dfrac{t_{\max}}{t_{\min}} \leq 2$.

For the boss vs wall: $R_1 = 2.67 > 2.0$ — **VIOLATION**.

For the wall vs rib: $R_2 = 1.20 < 2.0$ — **OK**.

### Step 3 — Propose a Correction
The boss pedestal is too thick. A common corrective action is to:
- **Hollow the boss** (core out the center),
- **Reduce the boss pedestal wall** to ≤ 2× the nominal wall = 6.0 mm, or
- **Add a cored pocket** in the boss base.

Corrected boss pedestal: **6.0 mm**, giving $R_1 = 6.0/3.0 = 2.0$ — **BOUNDARY ACCEPTABLE**.

$$\boxed{t_{\text{boss, corrected}} = 6.0 \text{ mm (with internal coring)}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Rib Design Validation

**Problem:**
A **PP chair seat** is to be designed with a nominal wall of **4.0 mm**. Calculate (a) the maximum rib thickness, (b) the minimum base fillet radius, and (c) the maximum rib height if the H/t ratio is limited to **3:1**.

### Step 1 — Rib Thickness
$$t_{\text{rib,max}} = 0.8 \times t_{\text{wall}} = 0.8 \times 4.0 = 3.2 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 2 — Base Fillet Radius
$$R_{\text{min}} = 0.5 \times t_{\text{wall}} = 0.5 \times 4.0 = 2.0 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 3 — Maximum Rib Height
$$H_{\text{max}} = 3 \times t_{\text{rib}} = 3 \times 3.2 = 9.6 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 4 — Summary
$$\boxed{t_{\text{rib}} = 3.2 \text{ mm}, \quad R = 2.0 \text{ mm}, \quad H_{\text{max}} = 9.6 \text{ mm}}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Drafting Strategy by Product

| Product Type | Preferred CAD View | Critical Feature | Material (Typical) | Draft Direction |
|--------------|---------------------|------------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| PET Bottle | Sectioned front view + isometric | Neck finish, thread, base | PET | Inside surface (core side) |
| Bucket with handle | Front view + side view + handle detail | Handle pivot, rim flange | PP | Outer surface (cavity side) |
| Pipe fitting (elbow) | Two-section views | Socket depth, chamfer | PVC / PP-R | Internal socket |
| Switch plate | Top view + section | Mounting holes, ribs | ABS / PC | Cavity side (away from core) |
| Injection-molded gear | Sectional + detail of teeth | Hub, keyway, tooth profile | POM / Nylon | Hub bore side |
| Container cap (threaded) | Sectional front view | Thread profile, knurl | HDPE / PP | Thread crest radius side |
| Thermoformed tray | Plan view + isometric | Lip, corner radii | PS / PET | Male mold side |
| Rotomolded tank | Elevation + section | Wall uniformity, fittings | LLDPE | Inside (rotational) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define shrinkage allowance in polymer product design. State the typical linear shrinkage range for Polypropylene (PP) and Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene (ABS).**

**Model Answer:**

Shrinkage allowance is the deliberate enlargement of the mold cavity dimension relative to the desired finished part dimension, to compensate for the volumetric contraction of the polymer as it cools from the ejection temperature to ambient room temperature.

Mathematically:
$$D_{\text{mold}} = \frac{D_{\text{part}}}{1 - S}$$

| Material | Linear Shrinkage (Typical) |
|----------|---------------------------|
| Polypropylene (PP) | **1.0% – 2.5%** |
| ABS | **0.4% – 0.7%** |

If a PP part is to measure **100 mm**, the mold must be cut at **101.0 mm to 102.5 mm** depending on the specific grade and processing conditions. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is a draft angle? Why is it essential in the design of injection-molded polymer parts? State its typical range.**

**Model Answer:**

A draft angle is a slight taper intentionally applied to the vertical (or near-vertical) walls of a molded polymer part, parallel to the direction of mold opening. It is essential because:

1. It allows the part to **eject cleanly** from the mold without scoring, dragging, or breaking.
2. It accommodates the **differential thermal contraction** between the part and the steel mold during cooling.
3. It reduces **ejection force** and prolongs **mold life**.

Typical range: **0.5° to 2° per side**, with **1°** being the most commonly used value in general industrial practice. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Choice-Based Full Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) Design and draft the orthographic views (front view, top view, and side sectional view) of a **1 L HDPE cylindrical container with a threaded neck finish (PET-bottle style)**, as per KTU drafting standards. Assume the following design data:** **(7 marks)**

- Nominal outer diameter at body: **85 mm**
- Nominal wall thickness: **1.2 mm**
- Neck outer diameter: **28 mm**
- Neck height: **18 mm**
- Total height of bottle: **240 mm**
- Material: **HDPE**, linear shrinkage: **2.0%**
- Draft angle: **1°**
- Add a typical screw-thread profile (Trapezoidal ISO 28 mm) at the neck finish.

**Model Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1: Compute the Shrinkage-Compensated Dimensions (1 mark)**

Using $D_{\text{mold}} = D_{\text{part}} / (1 - S) = D_{\text{part}} / 0.98$:

- Body diameter: $85 / 0.98 = 86.735 \text{ mm} \approx 86.74 \text{ mm}$
- Neck diameter: $28 / 0.98 = 28.571 \text{ mm} \approx 28.57 \text{ mm}$
- Height: $240 / 0.98 = 244.898 \text{ mm} \approx 244.90 \text{ mm}$

**Step 2: Compute the Draft-Induced Diameter Change (1 mark)**

For the body wall height (excluding neck) $h_{\text{body}} = 240 - 18 = 222 \text{ mm}$:
$$\Delta d = h_{\text{body}} \times \tan(1°) = 222 \times 0.017455 = 3.875 \text{ mm}$$

Top body diameter (after draft): $d_{\text{top}} = 85 + 2 \times 3.875 = 92.75 \text{ mm}$ (on the part). On the mold, this becomes $92.75 / 0.98 = 94.64 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 3: Generate the Front View (2 marks)**

- Draw the central vertical axis (centerline).
- Plot the body as a rectangle of width **86.74 mm (mold)** and height **222 mm (drafted)**.
- Plot the neck transition as a tapered shoulder from body top to neck base.
- Plot the neck finish as a rectangle of width **28.57 mm (mold)** and height **18 mm**.
- Add a **section line A-A** cutting vertically through the axis.

**Step 4: Generate the Top View (1 mark)**

- Draw two concentric circles: inner = inner body diameter $= 86.74 - 2(1.2) = 84.34 \text{ mm} \approx$ mold equivalent $= 84.34 / 0.98 = 86.06$ mm.
- Outer = outer body diameter = **86.74 mm (mold)**.
- Mark the parting line as a single horizontal trace.
- Add a center mark and four-way symmetry axes.

**Step 5: Generate the Sectional Side View (1 mark)**

- Re-draw the front view, but apply **sectional hatching** at 45° on the cut material.
- Reveal the **uniform wall thickness of 1.2 mm** throughout the body.
- Show the **internal neck bore**, **thread profile (cross-section)**, and **base dome** with a typical **3.0 mm radius** push-up.

**Step 6: Add GD&T and Notes (1 mark)**

- Apply a **cylindricity tolerance of 0.2 mm** on the body.
- Specify surface finish **Ra 0.8 μm** on the cosmetic outer surface.
- Note: "Material: HDPE, Density 0.95 g/cm³, Shrinkage 2.0%, Draft 1° applied on inside surface."

**Final Drawing Output:** Orthographic front + top + sectional side, with title block, BOM, and isometric.

---

**(b) A **PVC pipe elbow (90°) for a 50 mm nominal bore** is to be designed for a pressure rating of **6 bar**. Calculate the required wall thickness, draft the sectional view, and identify the parting line location. Given data:** **(7 marks)**

- PVC allowable hoop stress: $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 10 \text{ MPa}$
- Safety factor: $SF = 4$
- Outside diameter: $D_o = 60 \text{ mm}$
- Linear shrinkage of PVC: **0.5%**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the Required Wall Thickness Using the Barlow Equation (3 marks)**

The Barlow equation for thin-walled pressure pipes is:
$$t = \frac{P \cdot D_i}{2 \cdot \sigma_{\text{allow}} \cdot \eta}$$

For an elbow, the pressure-induced stress is amplified by a **flexibility factor** $\eta < 1$, typically $\eta = 0.6$ for an unreinforced elbow. Using the **derated** allowable stress:
$$\sigma_{\text{derated}} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}{SF} = \frac{10}{4} = 2.5 \text{ MPa}$$

Internal diameter: $D_i = 60 - 2t$.

Pressure: $P = 6 \text{ bar} = 0.6 \text{ MPa}$.

$$t = \frac{0.6 \times (60 - 2t)}{2 \times 2.5 \times 0.6}$$

$$t = \frac{0.6 \times (60 - 2t)}{3.0}$$

$$3.0 \, t = 0.6 \times 60 - 1.2 \, t$$

$$3.0 \, t + 1.2 \, t = 36$$

$$4.2 \, t = 36$$

$$t = 8.571 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Verify Using the Standard Dimension Ratio (SDR) (1 mark)**

$$SDR = \frac{D_o}{t} = \frac{60}{8.571} = 7.0$$

For a **6 bar** PVC pipe, the standard SDR is **13.6** (Class B) or **11** (Class C). Our calculated SDR of 7.0 is conservative (thicker wall), which is acceptable for an injection-molded elbow fitting.

**Step 3: Shrinkage Compensation (1 mark)**

$$t_{\text{mold}} = \frac{t}{1 - S} = \frac{8.571}{0.98} = 8.746 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Draft the Sectional View (1 mark)**

- Show the **two socket ends** (one at each 90° port).
- Indicate the **socket depth** (typically **$1.5 \times D_o = 90$ mm**).
- Apply a **1° draft** on the internal socket surface for ejection.
- Show the **chamfer (15° × 5 mm)** at the socket entry for pipe insertion guidance.
- Apply **sectional hatching** at 45° with a PVC material legend.

**Step 5: Identify the Parting Line (1 mark)**

The parting line should run along the **outer curve of the elbow's centerline radius** (the "spine" of the bend), so that the two mold halves close on a non-functional surface. The parting line must not coincide with the sealing socket surfaces.

**Final Output:** A fully dimensioned sectional view of the PVC elbow fitting.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) Explain the "Uniform Wall Thickness Rule" in polymer product design. For an **ABS electronic enclosure** with a base wall of **3.0 mm** and a battery compartment boss of **8.0 mm**, determine whether the design is moldable. If not, propose a corrected design.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Uniform Wall Thickness Rule states that the ratio of the thickest section to the thinnest section in a polymer part should not exceed **2:1** (and ideally 1.5:1). This is because thicker sections cool slower than thinner sections, leading to:
- Sink marks on the cosmetic face opposite the thick section,
- Internal voids,
- Differential shrinkage and warpage,
- Higher residual stress.

**Step 1: Apply the Rule (1 mark)**
$$R = \frac{t_{\text{boss}}}{t_{\text{wall}}} = \frac{8.0}{3.0} = 2.67 > 2.0$$

The design **violates** the rule.

**Step 2: Identify the Failure Mode (1 mark)**
A thick boss on a thin wall will cause a **sink mark** on the outer cosmetic surface of the enclosure, which is unacceptable for a consumer electronic product.

**Step 3: Propose Corrected Design (3 marks)**

**Correction A — Core Out the Boss:** Reduce the boss pedestal to **6.0 mm** (2× wall) and add an internal cored pocket of **4.0 mm** diameter, leaving a wall of **1.0 mm** around the cored hole.

**Correction B — Use a Gusseted Web:** Replace the solid boss pedestal with a **3-armed web structure**, each web having thickness = $0.7 \times 3.0 = 2.1$ mm and length = 12 mm.

**Correction C — Use a Locating Pin:** Replace the boss with a **heat-staked brass insert** after molding, avoiding the thick boss entirely.

**Recommended Solution:** A combination of Correction A + Correction B — cored boss with gussets — gives the optimal balance of moldability, stiffness, and cost.

**Step 4: Verify Corrected Design (1 mark)**
$$R_{\text{corrected}} = \frac{6.0}{3.0} = 2.0 \quad \text{(at the limit, acceptable with coring)}$$

**Step 5: Drafting Implication (1 mark)**
The corrected boss must be drafted at **1°** on all vertical surfaces, with a **base fillet of R = 1.5 mm** (0.5 × wall). The cored pocket must have a **draft of 1°** applied to its internal walls.

**Final Output:** Revised 2D drawing showing the cored, gusseted boss with proper draft and fillet.

---

**(b) A **PP living-hinge cap for a 100 mm diameter container** is to be designed. The hinge must survive **10,000 open/close cycles** without failure. Specify (i) the hinge geometry (thickness, length, radius), (ii) the material and processing notes, and (iii) draft the hinge detail in CAD.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Hinge Geometry Selection (2 marks)**

For a **polypropylene living hinge**, the standard design is a **thin, flexible web** with a generous radius on both sides. The hinge thickness $t_h$ is typically:

$$t_h = (0.25 \text{ to } 0.50) \times t_{\text{wall}}$$

If the cap wall is **2.0 mm**, then $t_h = 0.5 \text{ mm}$ (aggressive but standard for PP).

Hinge length $L_h$: typically **1.5 × to 2 ×** the wall thickness $= 3$ to $4$ mm.

Hinge radius $R_h$: at the hinge root, $R_h = 0.5 \times t_{\text{wall}} = 1.0 \text{ mm}$ to prevent stress concentration.

**Step 2: Material and Processing (2 marks)**

- **Material: Polypropylene homopolymer (PP-H)** with a melt flow index (MFI) of **8–12 g/10 min**.
- The hinge must be **molded in a single shot** with a **weld line-free** flow path. This is achieved by placing the hinge on the **parting line** so the polymer flow does not have to cross it.
- **Mold temperature:** 40–60 °C (to keep the hinge thin section amorphous and ductile).
- **Cooling time** at the hinge: must be **deliberately uniform** to avoid brittleness.

**Step 3: Drafting the Hinge Detail (2 marks)**

In CAD:
1. Create a **partial section view** through the cap and the hinge.
2. Show the cap wall at **2.0 mm**, transitioning to the hinge web at **0.5 mm**, with a fillet radius of **1.0 mm** on each side.
3. Indicate the **knock-out pin imprint** on the inside of the cap (to be located **away from the hinge**).
4. Add a **detail view** (scale 4:1 or 5:1) of the hinge cross-section, with dimensions for $t_h$, $L_h$, and $R_h$.

**Step 4: Cycle Life Justification (1 mark)**

PP living hinges can survive **> 1 million cycles** when the bending strain is kept below 0.5% per cycle, which corresponds to the geometry above. The **10,000-cycle** requirement is conservative and easily met.

**Final Output:** CAD detail of the PP living-hinge cap with dimensions and material notes.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in PCPOL308 — Module 2:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting shrinkage compensation (–2 to –3 marks):** Students often draw the *finished part dimensions* on the mold drawing, forgetting that the mold cavity must be larger. Always state and apply the shrinkage factor explicitly.
> 2. **No draft angle on vertical walls (–1 to –2 marks):** If the front view of a container shows perfectly parallel vertical walls, the examiner will mark it as "non-moldable." Apply at least **1°** of draft to every wall that is parallel to the mold opening direction.
> 3. **Confusing inside vs outside draft (–1 mark):** Draft direction matters. The *core-forming* surface (inside of a cup) and the *cavity-forming* surface (outside of a cup) require different draft strategies. Be explicit about which surface is drafted.
> 4. **Missing the sectional view (–2 marks):** For hollow polymer products, a sectional view is **mandatory** in KTU valuation. The examiner cannot assess internal wall thickness, neck bore, or thread profile without it.
> 5. **Wrong GD&T application (–1 to –2 marks):** Do not put general ±0.5 mm tolerances on every dimension. Use **specific tolerances only on critical-to-function** dimensions, and reference ASME Y14.5 if any geometric tolerance is used.
> 6. **No title block, no material note, no scale indication (–1 mark):** A KTU drawing without a title block is **incomplete**. Always include: Title, Name, Roll No, Date, Scale, Material, Shrinkage, Draft, Drawing No.
> 7. **Hatching error in sections (–1 mark):** Hatching must be **uniformly spaced, at 45°**, and **must not pass through** holes, ribs, or fasteners.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for PCPOL308 — Module Experiment 2:**

- [ ] **Shrinkage formula** (mold dimension from part dimension): $D_{\text{mold}} = D_{\text{part}} / (1 - S)$.
- [ ] **Typical linear shrinkage** for PE, PP, PS, ABS, PC, Nylon, POM — memorize the table.
- [ ] **Draft angle** range: **0.5° to 2°**, with **1°** as default.
- [ ] **Draft-induced diameter change** at height $h$: $\Delta d = h \cdot \tan \alpha$.
- [ ] **Wall thickness uniformity rule**: $t_{\max}/t_{\min} \leq 2$ (1.5 ideal).
- [ ] **Rib thickness rule**: $t_{\text{rib}} = (0.5 \text{ to } 0.8) \cdot t_{\text{wall}}$.
- [ ] **Rib base fillet**: $R = (0.5 \text{ to } 0.8) \cdot t_{\text{wall}}$.
- [ ] **Rib H/t ratio**: $\leq 3:1$ (5:1 max with proper base radius).
- [ ] **Boss outer diameter**: $D_{\text{boss}} \geq 2.5 \cdot d_{\text{hole}}$.
- [ ] **Barlow equation** for pressure pipes: $t = P \cdot D_i / (2 \sigma_{\text{allow}} \eta)$.
- [ ] **SDR (Standard Dimension Ratio)**: $SDR = D_o / t$.
- [ ] **Parting line** must lie on a non-aesthetic and non-functional surface.
- [ ] **Living hinge** for PP: $t_h \approx 0.25 t_{\text{wall}}$, placed on the **parting line**.
- [ ] **CAD views** required for any hollow product: **Front + Top + Sectional Side + Isometric**.
- [ ] **Title block** must contain: Material, Shrinkage, Draft, Scale, Drawing No.
- [ ] **GD&T** for cylindrical bodies: cylindricity ±0.1 to ±0.2 mm.
- [ ] **Process selection** decides drafting approach:
  - *Injection molding* → 2-plate or 3-plate mold, single or multi-cavity.
  - *Blow molding* → parison die, pinch-off, blow pin.
  - *Extrusion* → die land, calibrator, vacuum tank.
  - *Thermoforming* → male or female mold, vacuum holes.
  - *Rotomolding* → split mold, parting on non-aesthetic side.
- [ ] **Hatching** in section: uniform 45°, spacing 2–3 mm, never through holes.
- [ ] **Software output**: PDF + native CAD file (`.SLDPRT`, `.PRT`, `.STEP`) for the lab record.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Draft the part, not the dream."* Every line you draw in the CAD package is a promise to the polymer, the mold, the operator, and the end-user. If your drawing cannot be manufactured, your design has failed — no matter how elegant the geometry looks on the screen.
